title: AiReviewViewModel.pushFinding — service error path and already-pushed/discarded guard untested
description: pushFinding has three distinct guard/error branches that have zero test coverage: (1) the early-return guard `if (finding.pushed || finding.discarded) return false` (lines 63-64), (2) the service failure path `if (result.isError) { lastError = …; fire(); return false }` (lines 83-87), and (3) the pushAllKept() iterator (lines 100-109) which delegates to pushFinding but is not tested at all. The happy-path tests cover only the service-call dispatch (addPrComment / addInlineComment), not what the VM does when the push fails at the API layer or when findings are already archived.
suggestedFix: In AiReviewViewModelTest, add: (1) a test that calls pushFinding with a finding where pushed=true and asserts the return value is false and service is never called; (2) a test where service.addPrComment returns ToolResult.error("network error") and asserts vm.lastError is set and the finding is NOT marked pushed; (3) a test for pushAllKept that mixes already-pushed, already-discarded, and fresh findings and verifies only the fresh ones are pushed. All are fully testable via MockK without the IntelliJ platform.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed by reading AiReviewViewModelTest.kt in full. The test class has 5 tests: refresh, pushFinding-general-success, pushFinding-inline-no-toHash, pushFinding-inline-with-toHash, and discard. None test the early-return guard at lines 63-64 (`if (finding.pushed || finding.discarded) return false`), none test the service error path at lines 83-87 (`if (result.isError)`), and `pushAllKept()` (lines 100-109) has zero coverage. These are behaviorally significant — callers inspect the return value and `lastError` to show UI error states. The missing paths are purely testable via MockK without any platform dependency.
recommendedAction: Add three tests to AiReviewViewModelTest: (1) pushFinding with pushed=true returns false without calling service; (2) service returns ToolResult(isError=true) → pushFinding returns false and vm.lastError is set; (3) pushAllKept with a mix of pushed/discarded/fresh findings verifies only fresh ones are pushed and the count is correct.

title: CommentsViewModel mutation error paths (postGeneralComment, reply, resolve, reopen) have no failure-path tests
description: Four mutation methods — postGeneralComment, reply, resolve, reopen — each have an explicit `if (result.isError) { lastError = …; fire(); return false }` branch (lines 83-86, 99-103, 112-116, 127-131). The CommentsViewModelTest covers the success path for postGeneralComment, reply, and resolve, but not a single failure case for any of the four. The `reopen()` method (lines 123-137) has no test at all (success or failure). These are the paths users hit when the Bitbucket token is expired, the PR is merged, or the server returns 403.
suggestedFix: Add to CommentsViewModelTest: (1) `postGeneralComment returns false and sets lastError when service returns error`; (2) `reply returns false and sets lastError on service error`; (3) `resolve returns false and sets lastError on service error`; (4) `reopen calls service reopenPrComment and refreshes on success`; (5) `reopen returns false and sets lastError on service error`. All are fully testable via MockK coEvery returning ToolResult(isError=true).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed by reading CommentsViewModelTest.kt in full. The file has 6 tests: refresh-success, refresh-error, postGeneralComment-success, resolve-success, reply-success, and PrCommentsUpdated event. There is no failure-path test for postGeneralComment, reply, or resolve, and `reopen()` (CommentsViewModel.kt lines 123-137) has zero tests — neither success nor failure. The error branches at lines 83-86, 99-103, 112-116, and 127-131 all set `lastError` and return false; callers rely on these to surface error state in the UI. All are testable with MockK coEvery returning ToolResult(isError=true).
recommendedAction: Add failure-path tests for postGeneralComment, reply, and resolve (each asserting lastError is set and return value is false), plus both success and failure tests for reopen().

title: PrListService.fetchDashboardPrs — 100-result cap boundary and per-role API error propagation untested
description: fetchDashboardPrs (lines 200-232) has two critical untested paths: (1) the `results.size < 100` cap — when exactly 100 results accumulate, pagination stops even if isLastPage=false; there is no test that verifies the capped list is returned instead of continuing to fetch. (2) when getDashboardPullRequests returns ApiResult.Error (line 225), the method silently breaks out of the loop and returns whatever was collected so far (possibly an empty list), with only a log.warn. No test verifies that a mid-pagination API error still returns the partial results collected on prior pages. The only PrListService test in the suite (PrListServiceEdtMutationTest) is a source-text guard for EDT dispatch, not a behaviour test.
suggestedFix: Add a standalone test class PrListServiceFetchDashboardTest at the BitbucketBranchClient seam (using MockWebServer, same pattern as BitbucketServiceImplCommitsPaginationTest): (1) queue 4 pages of 25 PRs (total 100) with isLastPage=false — assert exactly 100 results returned and no further HTTP request made; (2) queue page-1 success + page-2 ApiResult.Error — assert partial results from page-1 are returned and the method does not throw.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. PrListService.kt lines 200-232 implement `fetchDashboardPrs` with a `results.size < 100` cap and an error-break path (lines 224-229). The only existing test (PrListServiceEdtMutationTest) is a source-text pattern check for the EDT dispatch — it has no behavioral coverage whatsoever for fetchDashboardPrs. The 100-cap boundary (stop when results.size reaches 100 even if isLastPage=false) is untested, as is the partial-results return on mid-pagination ApiResult.Error. The suggested MockWebServer-based approach matches the existing BitbucketServiceImplCommitsPaginationTest pattern in the module.
recommendedAction: Add PrListServiceFetchDashboardTest using MockWebServer at the BitbucketBranchClient seam: (1) serve exactly 4 pages of 25 PRs with isLastPage=false, assert exactly 100 results and no further HTTP request; (2) serve page-1 success + page-2 error, assert partial page-1 results are returned without throwing.

title: PrReviewSessionRegistry — updateStatus on non-existent key is a silent no-op with no test; all() method also untested
description: updateStatus (lines 33-38) silently does nothing when the prId is not in the registry (`val existing = current[prId] ?: return@withLock`). This is the expected safety gate, but callers (e.g. AgentLoop completing a review for a session whose registry entry was deleted) see no error and get no indication the status was dropped. There is no test that calls updateStatus on a non-existent prId and asserts the state is unchanged. Additionally, the `all()` method (line 42) that returns all entries for display in the AI Review tab has zero coverage — not tested for empty registry, single entry, or multiple entries.
suggestedFix: Add to PrReviewSessionRegistryTest: (1) `updateStatus on unknown prId is a no-op — all() returns empty` (call updateStatus before any register, assert get returns null); (2) `all() returns all registered entries` (register 3 PRs, call all(), assert size=3 and all entries match); (3) `all() returns empty map on fresh registry`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed by reading PrReviewSessionRegistryTest.kt. The file has 4 tests: register-persists, get-unknown-null, register-overwrites, updateStatus-preserves. The `updateStatus` early-return at line 35 (`val existing = current[prId] ?: return@withLock`) when called on a non-existent key is not exercised by any test. The `all()` method at line 42 — used by the AI Review tab to display all sessions — has no test at all (not for empty, single, or multiple entries). These are low-risk to add and provide a safety net for the caller's assumption that unknown-ID calls are no-ops.
recommendedAction: Add: (1) test that updateStatus on unknown prId leaves the registry unchanged; (2) test that all() returns all registered entries (register N, all() returns size N); (3) test that all() returns empty map on fresh registry.

title: PrReviewSessionRegistry — corrupt/malformed JSON file silently returns emptyMap() with no test
description: readFile() uses `runCatching { json.decodeFromString<RegistryFile>(...) }.getOrElse { emptyMap() }` (lines 58-60). If the file on disk is corrupt (truncated write, manual edit, schema change from a plugin downgrade), every subsequent register/get/updateStatus silently overwrites history with an empty base — the in-flight write after a corrupt read destroys all existing entries. The `getOrElse { emptyMap() }` recovery is correct behaviour, but there is no test proving it does not crash or throw, and no test that verifies a register() call after a corrupt read produces a single-entry file (rather than a zero-entry one).
suggestedFix: Add to PrReviewSessionRegistryTest: (1) `get() returns null and does not throw when file is corrupt JSON` (write `{ not valid json }` to the registry path, then call get()); (2) `register() after corrupt file creates a fresh single-entry file` (pre-write corrupt JSON, then register a PR, assert get() finds it with correct sessionId and the file is valid JSON).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at PrReviewSessionRegistry.kt lines 58-60: `runCatching { json.decodeFromString<RegistryFile>(...) }.getOrElse { emptyMap() }`. The corrupt-read path is correct behaviour, but it has no test. The concern is valid: a register() call after a corrupt-file read starts from emptyMap(), discarding all prior entries. The PrReviewSessionRegistryTest uses @TempDir which makes it easy to pre-seed a corrupt file. No existing test writes corrupt JSON and verifies graceful recovery. Not a runtime bug — the code is correct — but the lack of a test means future changes to readFile() could silently regress this safety gate.
recommendedAction: Add two tests using @TempDir: (1) pre-write corrupt JSON to the registry path, call get() and assert null is returned without exception; (2) pre-write corrupt JSON, then register a PR, assert get() returns the newly registered entry and the file is valid JSON.

title: PrDescriptionGenerator diff-cap retry ladder — ContextOverflow and non-retryable Other paths untested
description: The generate() method (lines 49-137) has a 4-step DIFF_CAP_LADDER retry loop (lines 76-109). The loop has three distinct branch outcomes: (1) TextGenerationOutcome.Success — returns the text; (2) TextGenerationOutcome.ContextOverflow — continues with the next (halved) cap; (3) TextGenerationOutcome.Other — breaks and falls through to Tier 2. The existing PrDescriptionGeneratorTest covers only the internal `buildFallbackDescription` helper (Tier 3). No test exercises the ContextOverflow path that triggers cap halving, nor the Other path that skips remaining retry slots and falls to Tier 2. The Tier 2 no-diff fallback branch (lines 113-131) is also untested.
suggestedFix: Add PrDescriptionGeneratorLadderTest using a fake TextGenerationService injected via a test seam or by making generate() accept the service as a parameter: (1) `ContextOverflow on first cap retries with halved cap and succeeds`; (2) `ContextOverflow on all four caps falls through to Tier 2`; (3) `Other failure on first cap skips remaining ladder and falls to Tier 2`; (4) `Tier 2 failure (null aiResult) falls to Tier 3 fallback`. Since TextGenerationService.getInstance() is a global singleton, the tests should ideally use the testProvider injection seam pattern already established in TicketChipInputTest.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. PrDescriptionGenerator.kt lines 76-109 show the DIFF_CAP_LADDER for-loop with three branch outcomes: Success (return), ContextOverflow (continue), and Other (break). PrDescriptionGeneratorTest only covers the `internal fun buildFallbackDescription` (Tier 3). The ContextOverflow cap-halving path (line 100-103), the Other early-break path (lines 104-107), and the entire Tier 2 no-diff fallback (lines 112-131) are untested. The claimed severity of High is overstated — this is a coverage gap, not a known behavioral defect. The actual ladder logic is straightforward Kotlin control flow. Downgraded to Medium because the missing coverage is for non-trivial branches (ContextOverflow retry semantics and Tier 2 fallthrough) that could silently regress. The fix is not harmful; `generate()` uses `TextGenerationService.getInstance()` (line 62) with no injection seam, so tests would need to either use the `testProvider` pattern referenced in the suggestion or restructure the method to accept the service — neither breaks existing behavior.
recommendedAction: Add a test seam to `generate()` (e.g., an internal overload accepting TextGenerationService directly, as the suggestion describes for TicketChipInputTest), then add tests for ContextOverflow-then-success, all-overflow-falls-to-Tier2, Other-breaks-to-Tier2, and Tier2-null-falls-to-Tier3.

title: PrListService.setState — concurrent setState + refresh race: @Volatile currentState read under coroutineScope untested
description: setState (lines 81-84) writes `currentState` (a @Volatile var) and immediately launches a coroutine on Dispatchers.IO to call refresh(). refresh() reads `currentState` inside an async block (line 210). If setState("MERGED") fires while a prior refresh() coroutine is still mid-flight reading currentState="OPEN", the two coroutines race on a @Volatile field. A @Volatile field prevents torn reads but does not prevent stale reads between setState's write and refresh's coroutineScope launch boundary. The codebase has no test verifying that two rapid setState calls produce a single final consistent state rather than interleaved or stale reads.
suggestedFix: Add a source-text contract test (same pattern as PrListServiceEdtMutationTest) asserting that currentState is accessed only as a @Volatile var (not as a MutableStateFlow or AtomicReference). Additionally add a behavioural note that setState's Dispatchers.IO launch is inherently best-effort (no sequential ordering guarantee). A concrete behaviour test: call setState("OPEN") then setState("MERGED") in rapid succession with a mock client that records which state each refresh sees, and assert the final _myPrs emission was fetched using "MERGED" (not "OPEN").
verdictReasoning: The race condition described is real at the level of @Volatile semantics: `setState` writes `currentState` then launches a new coroutine; a prior refresh coroutine already reading `currentState` on Dispatchers.IO will see the old value. @Volatile prevents torn reads but not stale reads between the write and the new coroutine's first read. However, this is the intended design: the field is @Volatile precisely to provide a best-effort snapshot — each refresh invocation reads the field at its own pace, and the StateFlow emissions that result are eventually consistent. The consequence of a stale read is a one-cycle UI refresh showing the wrong state bucket, not data corruption or a crash. Severity is lower than claimed (Medium→Low) since this is a best-effort eventual-consistency model with no data loss. A behavioral test as described is valuable but the gap is cosmetic rather than a user-visible bug in normal usage.
recommendedAction: The source-text contract test asserting @Volatile is already implicitly satisfied by the existing code. A behavioral test (rapid double setState, assert final emission used the last state) would add confidence but is low priority. If tighter ordering is desired, replace @Volatile with an AtomicReference and use getAndSet, but this is an optional architectural improvement, not a bug fix.

title: BitbucketServiceImpl.listPrComments — onlyOpen and onlyInline post-fetch filters have no test
description: After pagination, listPrComments applies two client-side filters (lines 1020-1022): `if (onlyOpen) mapped = mapped.filter { it.state == PrCommentState.OPEN }` and `if (onlyInline) mapped = mapped.filter { it.anchor != null }`. The pagination tests (BitbucketServiceImplListPrCommentsPaginationTest) operate at the BitbucketBranchClient layer where these filters don't exist. The BitbucketServiceImpl is not unit-testable directly, but the filter logic is a pure transformation on a List<PrComment> that can be extracted and tested independently — or tested via a minimal wrapper that exercises the service layer filter independently of the client layer.
suggestedFix: Extract the two filter predicates as internal/companion functions (similar to how PrActionService exposes internal mutators) so they can be called directly in unit tests. Add tests: (1) `onlyOpen=true filters out RESOLVED comments`; (2) `onlyInline=true filters out comments with null anchor`; (3) `both flags combine via AND (intersection)`; (4) `both flags false returns all comments unfiltered`. Alternatively, add a source-text contract test verifying the exact filter expressions are present in the body of listPrComments.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Lines 1020-1022 of BitbucketServiceImpl.kt apply two pure client-side filters after pagination: `if (onlyOpen) mapped = mapped.filter { it.state == PrCommentState.OPEN }` and `if (onlyInline) mapped = mapped.filter { it.anchor != null }`. The pagination test (BitbucketServiceImplListPrCommentsPaginationTest) explicitly documents in its architecture note that it operates at the BitbucketBranchClient layer where these filters do not exist. No test anywhere verifies the filter logic. However, both predicates are trivially correct one-liners over a List — the OPEN state enum comparison and null-anchor check carry essentially no branching risk. Severity is better assessed as Low: these are pure-function coverage gaps on straightforward predicates, not behavioral bugs users encounter. The suggested fix (extract as internal companion functions and add 4 tests) is safe and does not harm any existing behavior.
recommendedAction: Add unit tests for the four filter combinations (onlyOpen, onlyInline, both, neither). The simplest approach is a small test helper that builds a list of PrComment stubs with varying states and anchors and invokes the filter logic inline — extraction of the predicates is optional since they are trivial lambdas.

title: BitbucketServiceImpl.toPrComment — enum valueOf fallback for unknown state/severity/lineType strings untested
description: The private toPrComment extension (lines 1315-1348) uses `runCatching { PrCommentState.valueOf(state) }.getOrDefault(PrCommentState.OPEN)` and identical patterns for severity, lineType, and fileType. If Bitbucket Server returns a new state value (e.g. "PENDING" introduced in a newer DC release) or a lineType the client doesn't recognise, the comment silently maps to OPEN/NORMAL/null. There is no test verifying this fallback for any of the four fields, nor a test that an unrecognised enum value does not throw.
suggestedFix: Extract toPrComment as an internal top-level function (same pattern as the mutators in PrActionService.kt) so it is unit-testable. Add tests: (1) `unknown state string falls back to OPEN without throwing`; (2) `unknown severity string falls back to NORMAL without throwing`; (3) `unknown lineType string falls back to null anchor lineType without throwing`; (4) `known state RESOLVED maps correctly`. These exercise a parsing path that runs on every listPrComments call and silently masks server version mismatches.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Lines 1315-1348 of BitbucketServiceImpl.kt show the private `toPrComment` extension using `runCatching { PrCommentState.valueOf(state) }.getOrDefault(PrCommentState.OPEN)` and identical patterns for severity (NORMAL fallback), lineType (null fallback via getOrNull), and fileType (null fallback via getOrNull). There is no test that passes an unknown state/severity/lineType string and asserts no exception plus the correct fallback. The patterns are correct and defensive — the `runCatching` wrapping means unknown values silently map to defaults at runtime. The risk is real but already mitigated by the fallback logic itself. Severity is Low rather than Medium: the fallback is already in place and the code is correct; the gap is solely test coverage, not a latent bug. Fixing by extracting `toPrComment` as an internal top-level function is safe and non-breaking.
recommendedAction: Promote `toPrComment` to an internal top-level function (same file), then add tests verifying (1) unknown state falls back to OPEN without throwing, (2) unknown severity falls back to NORMAL, (3) unknown lineType leaves anchor.lineType null, (4) known RESOLVED state maps correctly.

title: PrActionService.decline — first-attempt fetch failure path (getPullRequestDetail returns Error) untested
description: fetchAndDecline (lines 249-263) first calls getPullRequestDetail. If this GET fails (404 — PR deleted, 403 — permission revoked, timeout), it returns the error directly (line 258: `is ApiResult.Error -> return r`). The retry path in decline() (lines 215-242) checks for 409 specifically, so a 404 on the pre-fetch produces an error at `first` that is neither a 409 nor a success, and is returned to the caller via the `if (!is409)` path (lines 218-221). The existing PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest tests the 409 retry cycle and the successful two-step sequence, but not the case where the initial GET itself fails. This is a real user path when a PR is closed (404) between the time the PR list loaded and the user clicks Decline.
suggestedFix: Add to PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest: `decline returns error when getPullRequestDetail returns 404` (enqueue a 404 for the GET, assert the returned ApiResult is an Error with appropriate type, and assert that no POST to the decline endpoint was made using server.takeRequest()). This exercises the fetchAndDecline early return and confirms the caller receives a meaningful error rather than a silent no-op.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. PrActionService.kt lines 249-263 show `fetchAndDecline`: if `getPullRequestDetail` returns `ApiResult.Error`, it immediately returns `r` (line 257). The caller at lines 218-221 checks for 409 specifically — a 404/403 on the GET is not a 409 and so is returned to the caller as an error. PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest tests (1) `declinePullRequest sends version in query string`, (2) `declinePullRequest maps 409 to VALIDATION_ERROR`, (3) `getPullRequestDetail returns current version`, (4) `fetchAndDecline pattern succeeds when GET then POST returns 200`, and (5) the 409-then-success two-step. None of these enqueue a 4xx on the GET leg. The gap is real. However, the early return on GET failure is the correct and safe behavior: the service does not fire the POST. The risk is that this path is unexercised and could regress silently if the early return were accidentally removed. Severity is Low not Medium: the behavior is provably correct from static analysis; there is no ambiguity in the code path and no observed regression. The fix is harmless.
recommendedAction: Add to PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest: enqueue a 404 for getPullRequestDetail, call `client.getPullRequestDetail` directly and verify it returns ApiResult.Error, then verify (via server.requestCount) that declinePullRequest was never reached. This exercises the fetchAndDecline early-return and guards against future regressions.

title: MarkdownToHtml — unclosed code block, empty input, and adjacent list-then-heading transitions untested
description:
renderBody() (lines 28-96) has three untested edge cases: (1) an unclosed fenced code block (the while-loop ends with `if (inCodeBlock) html.append("")` on line 93 — no test verifies this safety-close fires and produces well-formed HTML rather than an unclosed `
`); (2) empty string input — no test verifies `convert("")` returns `` without null errors; (3) a list immediately followed by a heading (inList=true when `##` is encountered) — the `if (inList) { html.append(""); inList = false }` guard on line 63 is not tested for the case where the heading comes right after a list item with no blank line separator.
suggestedFix:
Add to MarkdownToHtmlTest: (1) `convert on empty string returns valid html/body wrapper without body content`; (2) `unclosed code block is auto-closed at end of document`; (3) `heading immediately after list item closes the ul and opens h2` — input `"- item\n## Heading"`, assert single `` before `

` in the output.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. MarkdownToHtml.kt line 93 shows `if (inCodeBlock) html.append("")` as the safety-close for unclosed fenced code blocks. Line 92 shows the matching list close. MarkdownToHtmlTest.kt covers 10 cases (wrapper, fragment, bold, italic, code block, links, headings, lists, HTML escaping) but none of the three claimed gaps: (1) unclosed code block auto-close, (2) empty string input producing valid html/body wrapper, (3) heading immediately after a list item closing the ul. All three gaps are real and the code paths are active lines. Severity Low is appropriate: this is pure formatting/rendering logic with no data loss or security impact. The fix is safe — adding these three tests cannot break any existing passing test.
recommendedAction:
Add three tests to MarkdownToHtmlTest: (1) `convert("")` returns `` without errors; (2) unclosed fenced code block produces well-formed `
…
` in output; (3) input `"- item\n## Heading"` produces exactly one `` before `

` in the output.

title: BitbucketServiceImpl.mergePullRequest — getPullRequestDetail failure on version pre-fetch untested
description: mergePullRequest (lines 709-739) fetches the current PR version before merging (lines 720-726): `val currentPr = api.getPullRequestDetail(...)`. If this GET fails (e.g. 404 because the PR was already merged, or 403), it returns early with an error (line 723-725). This is a user-visible failure path — when a user races another merge from the UI — but there is no test for it. The existing PrActionServiceModifyRetryTest covers the merge retry path via mergePullRequestWithRetry at the client layer, not the BitbucketServiceImpl.mergePullRequest GET-then-POST pattern.
suggestedFix: Add a test at the BitbucketBranchClient seam using MockWebServer: enqueue a 404 for the getPullRequestDetail GET call and assert that mergePullRequest returns a ToolResult with isError=true containing a descriptive error message, and that no POST to the merge endpoint was made.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. BitbucketServiceImpl.kt lines 720-726 show that `mergePullRequest` calls `api.getPullRequestDetail(...)` and returns a ToolResult error immediately if the GET fails (lines 723-725): `is ApiResult.Error -> return ToolResult(data = Unit, summary = "Error fetching PR #$prId for merge: ${currentPr.message}", isError = true, ...)`. PrActionServiceModifyRetryTest tests `mergePullRequestWithRetry` at the `BitbucketBranchClient` layer (lines 29, 252+) which takes a version parameter directly — there is no GET involved at that layer. Therefore the service-level GET-then-POST pattern in BitbucketServiceImpl.mergePullRequest is untested. The early return on GET failure is the correct behavior. Severity is Low rather than Medium: static analysis confirms the early return is correct and safe; the gap is coverage-only. The suggested fix (MockWebServer test enqueuing a 404 for the GET) is safe and does not affect production behavior.
recommendedAction: Add a test using MockWebServer: enqueue a 404 for the getPullRequestDetail GET call and assert that the returned ToolResult has isError=true with a meaningful message, and that no POST to the merge endpoint was made (server.requestCount == 1).
